iterationContext: Use this when: clinical picture is viral (no bloody stool, no high fever, no systemic toxicity, no recent antibiotics or travel), patient is not dehydrated or mildly dehydrated, able to tolerate oral fluids
notes: Antibiotics are NOT indicated for viral diarrhoea. ORS is the cornerstone of treatment. Loperamide reduces stool frequency and is appropriate for adults who need functional improvement (e.g. travelling, cannot stay home). Avoid loperamide if bloody stool or suspected invasive bacterial cause.
duration: Symptomatic treatment for 3–5 days. ORS continued until normal stool consistency returns.
redFlagsForPatient: Return immediately if: unable to keep any fluids down for more than 4 hours, blood in stool, severe abdominal pain, fever above 38.5°C, no urine passed for 8 hours, feeling very faint or dizzy on standing

iterationContext: Use this when: red flag features suggesting bacterial cause are present — bloody or mucoid stool, high fever above 38.5°C, known exposure to bacterial source, immunocompromised patient, or clinical judgement strongly suggests bacterial aetiology. Note: most adults with bacterial diarrhoea still recover without antibiotics — use clinical judgement
notes: Ciprofloxacin or azithromycin are used depending on local resistance patterns. Azithromycin preferred in areas with high fluoroquinolone resistance (including Egypt). Metronidazole added if amoebic or Giardia cause suspected — particularly after travel or contaminated water exposure. Always send stool culture before starting antibiotics if possible.
duration: Ciprofloxacin 3–5 days. Azithromycin 3 days. Metronidazole 5–7 days if protozoal cause.
redFlagsForPatient: Return immediately if: worsening despite antibiotics after 48 hours, blood in stool increasing, high fever not settling, severe dehydration signs
source: IDSA Clinical Infectious Diseases Guidelines, WHO Diarrhoea Treatment Guidelines 2023

iterationContext: Use this when: patient is a child. ORS is the primary treatment in all paediatric diarrhoea regardless of cause. Antibiotics only if clear bacterial cause with red flags. Loperamide is contraindicated under 12 years. Zinc supplementation recommended by WHO in children under 5.
notes: ORS volume: 50–100ml/kg over 3–4 hours for mild-moderate dehydration. Continue breastfeeding throughout. Resume normal feeding as soon as tolerated — do not withhold food. Zinc 20mg daily for 10 days in children under 5 (10mg daily under 6 months) — WHO recommendation for reducing severity and duration.
duration: ORS as needed until diarrhoea resolves. Zinc for 10 days regardless of clinical resolution.
redFlagsForPatient: Return immediately if: child becomes lethargic or unconscious, blood in stool, unable to drink ORS, sunken eyes, not passing urine for 6 hours, high fever, worsening
source: WHO/UNICEF Joint Statement on ORS and Zinc 2004, WHO IMCI guidelines 2014

iterationContext: Use this when: acute constipation in any adult, no rectal impaction on examination, able to take oral medication. Osmotic laxatives (lactulose, macrogol) are first-line per NICE and BSG — they work by drawing water into the bowel and are well tolerated.
notes: Advise concurrent lifestyle measures: increase fluid intake to 1.5–2 litres per day, increase dietary fibre, regular physical activity. Macrogol (polyethylene glycol) preferred over lactulose — faster onset, better tolerated, less bloating. Lactulose is acceptable alternative if macrogol unavailable.
duration: Use until bowel movements normalise — typically 3–7 days. Taper and stop when regular bowel habit re-established. Continue lifestyle measures long-term.
redFlagsForPatient: Return if: no bowel movement after 3 days of laxatives, severe abdominal pain or distension, blood in stool, vomiting, unable to pass gas
source: NICE CG61, British Society of Gastroenterology Constipation Guidelines 2022

iterationContext: Use this when: osmotic laxative alone has not produced a bowel movement within 48–72 hours, or patient prefers a more rapid-onset agent, or patient has hard impacted stool requiring peristaltic stimulation. Stimulant laxatives (bisacodyl, senna) act on the colonic wall to stimulate peristalsis.
notes: Stimulant laxatives can be combined with an osmotic laxative for additive effect. Bisacodyl suppository gives faster onset than oral (acts within 30–60 minutes) — useful when rapid relief needed. Do not use stimulant laxatives regularly for more than 1 week without reassessment — risk of dependence and electrolyte imbalance with prolonged use.
duration: Short-term use only — 3–5 days maximum without review. Switch back to osmotic laxative for maintenance.
redFlagsForPatient: Return if: severe cramping, no relief after 3 doses, blood in stool, abdominal distension worsening

iterationContext: Use this when: patient is pregnant. Constipation is extremely common in pregnancy due to progesterone reducing bowel motility. Macrogol is safe in all trimesters. Stimulant laxatives (especially senna) should be avoided in pregnancy due to potential uterine stimulation.
notes: Start with lifestyle: increase fluid intake, dietary fibre, gentle exercise. If lifestyle insufficient, macrogol is first-line safe option in all trimesters. Lactulose is also safe but less effective and causes more bloating. Avoid: senna (uterine stimulation risk, especially third trimester), castor oil (may induce labour). Glycerine suppositories are safe if needed for local softening.
duration: Ongoing as needed throughout pregnancy. Regular reassessment. Lifestyle modifications are primary long-term management.
redFlagsForPatient: Return if: severe abdominal pain, rectal bleeding, haemorrhoids worsening, no effect after 5 days of treatment, associated with vomiting
source: NICE CKS Constipation in Pregnancy 2023, BNF 2024

iterationContext: Use this when: classic viral URTI presentation, chest examination clear, no red flags, patient asking for something to help with cough and sleep. No antibiotic indicated. Antitussives reduce cough frequency and improve sleep quality without treating the cause.
notes: Dextromethorphan (centrally acting antitussive) is first-line for dry irritating cough. Guaifenesin (expectorant) helps when cough is productive — thins mucus. Many combination products contain both. Honey with warm water (1 teaspoon in warm water before bed) has evidence in adults and children over 1 year — suggest as adjunct. Steam inhalation and saline nasal rinse for nasal congestion component. Advise patient the cough may last 2–3 weeks even after other symptoms resolve — this is normal.
duration: Antitussives for 5–7 days. No benefit beyond 2 weeks. If cough persists beyond 3 weeks, reassess.
redFlagsForPatient: Return immediately if: difficulty breathing, chest pain, coughing up blood, high fever returning after initial improvement, unable to sleep flat
source: NICE NG120 2019, NICE CKS Acute Cough 2023

iterationContext: Use this when: acute URTI cough is associated with wheeze on auscultation, or cough is triggered by cold air, exercise, or talking — suggesting a post-viral or reactive airway component. This is common in patients with background asthma or undiagnosed reactive airways disease.
notes: Salbutamol (short-acting beta-2 agonist) relaxes bronchial smooth muscle — significantly reduces cough associated with bronchospasm. Use via metered-dose inhaler with spacer for best delivery. If patient responds well, consider formal asthma assessment at follow-up. Do not start inhaled corticosteroid for a single acute viral episode without confirmed diagnosis.
duration: Salbutamol as needed for 5–7 days. If requiring it more than three times per day, reassess for asthma diagnosis.
redFlagsForPatient: Return immediately if: wheeze worsening despite inhaler, breathing becomes laboured, lips or fingernails turning blue, salbutamol not helping within 10 minutes
source: NICE NG80 Asthma 2017 updated 2022, SIGN 158 Asthma 2019

iterationContext: Use this when: child presenting with cough as part of viral URTI. Over-the-counter cough medicines are not recommended in children under 6 years (MHRA guidance). Management is supportive. Honey safe over 1 year. Salbutamol only if wheeze confirmed on auscultation.
notes: Under 6 years: no cough suppressants. Honey 2.5ml before sleep for children over 1 year. Adequate fluids. Saline nasal drops for nasal congestion. Paracetamol or ibuprofen if fever or discomfort (age-appropriate doses). 6–12 years: simple linctus is safe but provides minimal evidence-based benefit — can be used for symptomatic comfort. Over 12 years: treat as adult. Salbutamol only if wheeze confirmed — never empirical.
duration: Supportive care for duration of illness — typically 7–14 days. No medication for longer than 7 days without reassessment.
redFlagsForPatient: Return immediately if: child working hard to breathe (ribs visible on breathing in), breathing very fast, makes high-pitched sound on breathing in (stridor), lips blue, very drowsy or difficult to wake, not drinking anything for 8 hours
source: MHRA Medicines for Children Cough Guidelines, NICE CKS Cough in Children 2023

iterationContext: Use this when: pain score 1–3, any cause (musculoskeletal, headache, post-procedure, dental), no contraindication to paracetamol. Paracetamol is the safest first-line analgesic — suitable in pregnancy, elderly, renal impairment, peptic ulcer history, and cardiovascular disease.
notes: Paracetamol must be dosed correctly — underdosing is common. 1g four times daily (up to 4g/day) is the full analgesic dose for adults over 50kg. Regular dosing is more effective than PRN for acute pain — advise patient to take it on a schedule, not wait for pain to peak. Advise patient not to take other products containing paracetamol simultaneously (many cold and flu medications contain it).
duration: 5–7 days for acute musculoskeletal. 2–3 days for post-procedural. Review if no improvement.
redFlagsForPatient: Return if: pain not improving after 48 hours, pain score increasing, new symptoms develop, fever develops with musculoskeletal pain
source: WHO Analgesic Ladder, BNF 2024, NICE CKS Analgesia 2023

iterationContext: Use this when: pain score 4–6, likely inflammatory component (musculoskeletal injury, dental pain, post-operative), no contraindication to NSAIDs. NSAIDs are more effective than paracetamol for inflammatory pain. Always check: no peptic ulcer history, normal renal function, no cardiovascular disease, not pregnant.
notes: Ibuprofen is the safest NSAID for short-term use. Always prescribe with food to reduce GI side effects. Add omeprazole or equivalent PPI if patient at risk: age over 65, previous peptic ulcer, concurrent corticosteroid or anticoagulant use. Diclofenac is more potent but has higher cardiovascular risk — avoid in patients with hypertension or cardiac disease. Naproxen preferred in patients with borderline cardiovascular risk due to relatively lower CV risk profile.
duration: Shortest effective course — typically 5–7 days. Avoid regular NSAIDs beyond 2 weeks without reassessment.
redFlagsForPatient: Return if: black or tarry stools, severe indigestion or epigastric pain, swelling of feet or ankles developing, pain not improving after 5 days
source: NICE CKS Analgesia 2023, BNF 2024, EMA NSAID safety review

iterationContext: Use this when: pain score 4–6 but NSAIDs contraindicated — peptic ulcer disease, severe renal impairment (eGFR <30), pregnancy, aspirin-sensitive asthma, cardiovascular disease with high risk. Paracetamol alone is insufficient and NSAIDs cannot be used. Codeine adds opioid component at step 2 of the WHO ladder.
notes: Codeine is a prodrug — 10% of patients are poor metabolisers and get no benefit. 1–2% are ultra-rapid metabolisers and can get toxic plasma levels — avoid in: breastfeeding mothers (risk to infant), children under 12 (contraindicated), children under 18 after tonsillectomy/adenoidectomy. Constipation is the most common side effect — advise patient and consider concurrent laxative if using for more than 3 days. Drowsiness and nausea are common on initiation.
duration: Maximum 3 days for acute pain. Opioids should not be continued beyond 5 days for acute non-cancer pain without specialist input.
redFlagsForPatient: Return if: excessive drowsiness, confusion, constipation not relieved by laxatives, no pain improvement after 48 hours — the pain source needs re-evaluation
source: WHO Analgesic Ladder, BNF 2024, NICE CKS Analgesia 2023, MHRA codeine safety update

iterationContext: Use this when: patient is pregnant with mild-moderate acute pain. Paracetamol is the only analgesic safe in all trimesters. NSAIDs are contraindicated in the third trimester (premature closure of ductus arteriosus) and should be avoided throughout pregnancy if possible. Codeine requires caution — avoid in breastfeeding.
notes: Paracetamol is safe in all three trimesters at standard doses. Recent epidemiological studies raised concern about prolonged use of paracetamol in pregnancy — advise to use the lowest effective dose for the shortest time and not to use continuously for more than 3 days without obstetric input. NSAIDs: absolutely contraindicated from 28 weeks onwards. Avoid in first trimester (theoretical risk). If pain is severe and not controlled, refer to obstetrics.
duration: Shortest possible duration. Daily regular use in pregnancy requires obstetric review after 3 days.
redFlagsForPatient: Return if: pain persisting despite paracetamol, abdominal pain or cramping (may not be musculoskeletal), reduced fetal movements, any new symptoms
source: NICE CKS Analgesia in Pregnancy 2023, RCOG Green-top guidance, BNF 2024

iterationContext: Use this when: uncomplicated lower UTI in a non-pregnant adult female, no allergy to trimethoprim or sulfonamides, no recent trimethoprim use (local resistance rates acceptable below 20%), not immunocompromised. Trimethoprim is first-line per NICE due to narrow spectrum, low cost, and excellent oral bioavailability.
notes: Trimethoprim resistance is rising — check local antibiogram if available. If patient has used trimethoprim in the last 3 months, switch to nitrofurantoin or pivmecillinam. Advise patient: symptoms should improve within 48 hours. Encourage fluid intake (2 litres per day). Urinary alkalinising agents (e.g. sodium bicarbonate sachets) can relieve dysuria while waiting for antibiotic to work — optional adjunct. No need for routine MSU in uncomplicated first episode.
duration: 3 days (NICE recommends 3-day course for uncomplicated lower UTI — as effective as 7 days with fewer side effects)
redFlagsForPatient: Return if: fever develops or worsens, pain moves to lower back or side, symptoms not improving after 48 hours, symptoms return within 2 weeks of finishing antibiotics

iterationName: Alternative — Nitrofurantoin (First-line in Pregnancy or Trimethoprim Resistance)
iterationContext: Use this when: patient is pregnant (nitrofurantoin is preferred in first and second trimester), OR known trimethoprim resistance or recent trimethoprim use in the last 3 months, OR trimethoprim allergy. Note: nitrofurantoin should NOT be used if eGFR less than 45 ml/min — insufficient drug reaches the urine.
notes: Nitrofurantoin modified-release (100mg twice daily) is preferred over standard release (50mg four times daily) — equivalent efficacy with better tolerability. Takes food with each dose to improve absorption and reduce nausea. Turns urine dark yellow/brown — warn patient — this is harmless. Pregnancy: safe in first and second trimester. Avoid at term (from 36 weeks) — risk of neonatal haemolytic anaemia. Do not use in patients with G6PD deficiency.
duration: 7 days for most presentations. 3 days acceptable for uncomplicated lower UTI in non-pregnant women only if modified-release formulation used.
redFlagsForPatient: Return if: pulmonary symptoms develop (rare but serious — nitrofurantoin pulmonary reaction), fever or loin pain develops, symptoms not improving at 48 hours
source: NICE NG109 UTI in Adults 2022, NICE UTI in Pregnancy CKS 2023

iterationName: Treatment Failure or Complicated UTI — Ciprofloxacin
iterationContext: Use this when: culture result shows trimethoprim and nitrofurantoin resistance, or clinical picture suggests upper UTI / pyelonephritis (fever, loin pain, rigors) in a patient not requiring hospital admission. Ciprofloxacin has excellent urinary bioavailability and broad gram-negative coverage.
notes: Reserve fluoroquinolones for treatment failure or complicated UTI — WHO classifies them as critically important antimicrobials. Do not use as first-line for simple UTI to preserve efficacy. If patient has pyelonephritis features, longer course is required. Ciprofloxacin interacts with dairy products — advise to take 2 hours before or 6 hours after dairy. Tendon rupture risk — caution in elderly and patients on corticosteroids.
duration: Lower UTI (treatment failure): 7 days. Upper UTI / pyelonephritis (outpatient): 7 days. If symptoms severe or not improving at 48 hours — admit.
redFlagsForPatient: Return immediately if: fever above 38.5°C, pain spreading to flank or back, rigors, vomiting, unable to keep fluids down, feeling very unwell
source: NICE NG109 UTI in Adults 2022, EAU Urological Infections Guidelines 2023
